summary: Gruber publishes the full text of Marco Rubio's State Department directive reverting from Calibri back to Times New Roman, arguing that the New York Times mischaracterized the memo as culture-war anti-woke posturing. He contends the memo is actually a focused, well-argued case about aesthetics and formality in diplomatic correspondence. Gruber dismantles the Biden-era accessibility justification for adopting Calibri, noting that OCR claims about serif fonts are nonsense and that document remediation cases didn't decline after the switch. He praises Rubio for backing his argument with actual numbers showing the Calibri change was purely performative. While acknowledging one objectionable aside about DEIA initiatives, Gruber concludes the memo is otherwise fair and informative.
key_points: The New York Times selectively quoted only 56 words from a 950-word memo to frame it as an anti-woke culture war move, when the memo is primarily about aesthetics and formality
The Biden-era claim that Calibri improved accessibility over Times New Roman for OCR and screen readers is provably false — no modern OCR software has trouble with Times New Roman
Rubio's memo includes actual data: document remediation cases stayed flat (1,192 vs 1,193) and costs increased by $145,000 after the Calibri switch, undermining the accessibility rationale
Gruber emphasizes the importance of publishing original source documents rather than cherry-picking excerpts, and publishes the full memo himself
The Calibri change exemplifies the worst kind of accessibility theater — visible and feel-good but with zero practical benefit
money_quotes: It drives me nuts when news sites in possession of a statement or original document do not make the full original text available, even if only in a link at the bottom, and choose only to quote short excerpts.
Rubio's memo wasn't merely 'mostly framed as a matter of clarity and formality in presentation'. That's entirely what the memo is about.
The bit here about OCR is utter nonsense, a voodoo belief.
No OCR or screen-reader software in use today has any problem whatsoever with Times New Roman. That's just made-up nonsense.
The change to Calibri was the worst kind of accessibility effort: one that was founded on nothing more than feel-good performance.
It was a change everyone could see and notice, but one that had no practical benefit whatsoever.
Good on Rubio for rescinding a bad decision, and even better for doing so with a fair and informative explanation.
key_insight: The New York Times misrepresented a substantive, data-backed memo about typography and formality as culture-war posturing, while the real story is that the Biden-era switch to Calibri was accessibility theater with zero measurable benefit.
